messages.content: What is the waiting period for cataract surgery under health insurance in India?

Under IRDAI guidelines, the standard waiting period for cataract surgery is 2 years from the policy start date. This is a pre-existing disease (PED) waiting period that applies to most individual health policies. Some insurers offer policies with reduced waiting periods (1 year) at a higher premium. Always check your specific policy document — the waiting period clause is usually in Section 4 or the exclusions section.
My father has diabetes. Will his health insurance cover diabetes-related complications?

Diabetes is classified as a pre-existing disease (PED) under IRDAI rules. Here is what typically applies:

• **Waiting period**: 2-4 years before diabetes and its complications are covered
• **After waiting period**: Most complications (retinopathy, nephropathy, neuropathy) are covered
• **Exceptions**: Some policies cover insulin and OPD costs from day 1 at higher premiums

Key tip: Buy health insurance early, before diabetes is diagnosed, as it will then be covered from the start. If already diagnosed, declare it honestly — non-disclosure can lead to claim rejection.
What is Ayushman Bharat? Who is eligible?

Ayushman Bharat — Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana (PM-JAY) is India's largest health insurance scheme. Key details:

**Coverage**: ₹5 lakh per family per year for secondary and tertiary hospitalisation

**Eligibility** (based on SECC 2011 data):
• Rural families: those in kutcha houses, landless labourers, daily wage workers, SC/ST households, families with no earning adult member
• Urban families: rag pickers, domestic workers, street vendors, construction workers, sanitation workers

**How to check**: Visit pmjay.gov.in or call helpline 14555 with your Aadhaar number

**Hospitals**: Over 25,000 empanelled hospitals across India — treatment is cashless and paperless
What is the generic alternative for Crocin?

Crocin's active ingredient is **Paracetamol (Acetaminophen) 500mg**.

Generic alternatives available at Jan Aushadhi stores:
• Paracetamol 500mg tablet — ₹1-2 per tablet (vs ₹2-4 for Crocin)
• Available at all government hospitals and Jan Aushadhi Kendras

**Savings**: Up to 50-70% cheaper than branded versions

Both branded and generic contain the same active ingredient at the same dose — the quality is equivalent. The difference is only in the brand name and packaging.
Can I take ibuprofen with blood pressure medication?

This is an important drug interaction to be aware of:

**Ibuprofen + Blood Pressure Medications**: Ibuprofen (Brufen, Combiflam) can **reduce the effectiveness** of many BP medications, including:
• ACE inhibitors (Enalapril, Ramipril)
• ARBs (Losartan, Telmisartan)
• Beta-blockers (Atenolol, Metoprolol)
• Diuretics (Furosemide, Hydrochlorothiazide)

**Risk**: NSAIDs like ibuprofen cause sodium and water retention, which raises blood pressure and counteracts your medication.

**What to use instead**: Paracetamol (Crocin, Dolo) is generally safer for pain relief if you have hypertension.

⚠️ Please consult your doctor before combining these medications. This is general information, not a substitute for medical advice.
मेरे lab report में hemoglobin 9.5 g/dL है। क्या यह normal है?

नहीं, 9.5 g/dL hemoglobin **सामान्य से कम** है।

**Normal ranges:**
• पुरुष: 13.5 – 17.5 g/dL
• महिला: 12.0 – 15.5 g/dL
• बच्चे: 11.0 – 16.0 g/dL (उम्र के अनुसार)

**9.5 g/dL का मतलब है**: मध्यम एनीमिया (Moderate Anaemia)

**सामान्य कारण:**
• आयरन की कमी (सबसे आम — खासकर महिलाओं में)
• विटामिन B12 या फोलिक एसिड की कमी
• पुरानी बीमारी

**क्या करें:**
1. डॉक्टर से मिलें — वे Iron studies, B12, और Ferritin test करवाएंगे
2. आयरन से भरपूर खाना खाएं: पालक, चना, गुड़, राजमा
3. विटामिन C के साथ आयरन खाने से अवशोषण बढ़ता है

⚠️ यह जानकारी सामान्य मार्गदर्शन के लिए है। कृपया अपने डॉक्टर से सलाह लें।
What documents are needed to file a health insurance claim?

For a **reimbursement claim** (you paid first, now claiming back):

**Mandatory documents:**
1. Duly filled claim form (from insurer's website)
2. Original hospital discharge summary
3. Original bills and receipts (itemised)
4. Doctor's prescription and consultation notes
5. Lab reports and diagnostic test results
6. Policy document / health card
7. Cancelled cheque (for NEFT reimbursement)
8. Photo ID proof (Aadhaar/PAN)

**For cashless claims** (hospital processes directly):
• Only pre-authorisation form + health card needed at admission
• Hospital's TPA desk handles the rest

**Tips:**
• Submit within 30 days of discharge (most policies)
• Keep photocopies of everything before submitting originals
• Note your claim reference number for follow-up
What is the room rent limit in health insurance?

Room rent limits are one of the most common reasons for partial claim settlement. Here is how it works:

**Common room rent caps:**
• Basic policies: 1% of sum insured per day (e.g., ₹500/day on a ₹5L policy)
• Mid-range: 2% of sum insured per day
• Some policies: No room rent cap (best option)

**Why it matters (proportionate deduction):**
If your limit is ₹3,000/day but you stayed in a ₹6,000/day room, the insurer may settle only 50% of your **total bill** — not just the room difference. This includes doctor fees, medicines, and procedures.

**IRDAI ruling**: Proportionate deduction on associated medical expenses is allowed but must be clearly stated in the policy.

**Recommendation**: Choose a policy with no room rent sub-limit or at least 2% of sum insured. Always check this before admission.
I have chest pain. What should I do?

🚨 **If your chest pain is severe, spreading to your arm/jaw, or you feel breathless — call 112 immediately. This may be a heart attack.**

For chest pain, do not wait. Go to the nearest emergency room.

**Call emergency services:**
• National Emergency: **112**
• Ambulance: **108** (free across India)

**Do not drive yourself.** Have someone take you or call an ambulance.

This is not something to manage at home or wait to see a doctor about tomorrow.
What is the difference between Atorvastatin and Rosuvastatin?

Both are statins used to lower cholesterol, but they differ in potency and metabolism:

| Feature | Atorvastatin | Rosuvastatin |
|---------|-------------|-------------|
| Brand names | Lipitor, Atorva | Crestor, Rozavel |
| Potency | Moderate | Higher (more LDL reduction per mg) |
| Metabolism | CYP3A4 (liver) | Less liver metabolism |
| Drug interactions | More (grapefruit, some antibiotics) | Fewer |
| Kidney excretion | Less | More |

**In practice**: Rosuvastatin is often preferred for patients needing aggressive LDL reduction or those on multiple medications. Atorvastatin is cheaper and equally effective for most patients.

**Generic tip**: Generic atorvastatin 10mg costs ~₹15-30/month at Jan Aushadhi stores vs ₹200+ for branded.

⚠️ Do not switch statins without consulting your cardiologist or physician.
What is portability in health insurance?

Health insurance **portability** allows you to switch from one insurer to another without losing the benefits you have accumulated.

**What you retain on porting:**
• Waiting period credit (PED waiting period already served)
• No-Claim Bonus (NCB) accumulated
• Continuity of coverage

**IRDAI portability rules:**
• You must apply 45 days before policy renewal
• New insurer cannot reject you solely based on previous claims
• Sum insured can be increased (fresh waiting period applies only to increased amount)

**How to port:**
1. Get portability form from new insurer
2. Submit with existing policy documents and claim history
3. New insurer must decide within 15 days

**When to port:**
• If your current insurer keeps raising premiums
• If you are unhappy with claim settlement ratio
• If you want better coverage features
My blood sugar fasting is 118 mg/dL. Is this diabetes?

A fasting blood glucose of 118 mg/dL falls in the **pre-diabetes** range.

**Classification (WHO / ADA standards):**
• Normal: Less than 100 mg/dL
• Pre-diabetes (Impaired Fasting Glucose): 100–125 mg/dL ← You are here
• Diabetes: 126 mg/dL or above (confirmed on 2 separate tests)

**What pre-diabetes means:**
You have a higher-than-normal risk of developing Type 2 diabetes, but it is **reversible** with lifestyle changes.

**Action steps:**
1. **Diet**: Reduce refined carbs, sugar, white rice; increase vegetables, pulses, whole grains
2. **Exercise**: 30 minutes of brisk walking, 5 days a week
3. **Weight**: Losing even 5-7% of body weight significantly reduces risk
4. **Follow-up**: HbA1c test to see your 3-month average

Please see your doctor for a full assessment. Early intervention can prevent diabetes entirely.
What is co-payment in health insurance?

**Co-payment (co-pay)** means you pay a fixed percentage of every claim, and the insurer pays the rest.

**Example:** 20% co-pay on a ₹1,00,000 bill:
• You pay: ₹20,000
• Insurer pays: ₹80,000

**Common co-pay scenarios in India:**
• Senior citizen policies: 10-20% co-pay (very common)
• Zone-based co-pay: If you get treated in a higher-cost city than your base zone
• Specific treatments or hospitals
• Government employee policies (CGHS)

**Co-pay vs Deductible:**
• Co-pay: A percentage of each claim
• Deductible: A fixed amount you pay first, insurer covers the rest

**Tip:** Policies with co-pay have lower premiums. For young, healthy individuals this can be a good trade-off. For elderly or those with chronic conditions, prefer zero co-pay policies even if more expensive.
Which medicines are available free at government hospitals in India?

Several free medicine programs exist across India:

**1. Jan Aushadhi Kendras (Pradhan Mantri Bhartiya Janaushadhi Pariyojana)**
• Over 10,000 stores across India
• Generic medicines at 50-90% lower prices than branded
• Not free, but very affordable

**2. Free medicines at government hospitals:**
• All AIIMS hospitals: Free medicines for inpatients
• State government hospitals: Free medicines from essential medicines list (varies by state)
• PHCs (Primary Health Centres): Free for BPL card holders

**3. Ayushman Bharat (PM-JAY):**
• Free medicines for all covered treatments at empanelled hospitals
• Includes high-cost drugs like chemotherapy, cardiac drugs

**4. State-specific schemes:**
• Tamil Nadu: Free medicines in all government hospitals (comprehensive list)
• Rajasthan: Mukhyamantri Nishulk Dava Yojana
• Delhi: Free medicines at Mohalla Clinics and Delhi government hospitals

Visit your nearest government hospital or PHC with your Aadhaar and ration card to check eligibility.
What is no-claim bonus in health insurance?

**No-Claim Bonus (NCB)** is a reward for not making any insurance claims during a policy year.

**How NCB works:**
• Each claim-free year, your sum insured increases by a fixed percentage (typically 5-50%)
• The premium usually stays the same or increases only slightly
• Accumulated over multiple years

**Example:**
• Base sum insured: ₹5,00,000
• Year 1 (no claim): Sum insured becomes ₹5,50,000 (10% NCB)
• Year 2 (no claim): Sum insured becomes ₹6,00,000 or ₹6,05,000
• Some insurers offer up to 100% NCB over 5 years

**NCB on portability:** You retain NCB if you port within 45 days of renewal.

**Types:**
• **Cumulative NCB**: Sum insured increases each year
• **NCB Protect**: Add-on that preserves NCB even if you make 1-2 claims

NCB protect riders are worth buying if you have senior family members on the policy.
What is HbA1c and what is a normal value?

**HbA1c (Glycated Haemoglobin)** measures your average blood sugar over the past 2-3 months. It tells you how well diabetes is controlled without requiring fasting.

**Normal ranges:**
• Normal (no diabetes): Below 5.7%
• Pre-diabetes: 5.7% – 6.4%
• Diabetes: 6.5% or above (confirmed on repeat test)
• Well-controlled diabetes: Below 7.0% (target for most diabetics)
• Poorly controlled: Above 8.0% (higher risk of complications)

**Why it is better than fasting glucose:**
• Not affected by what you ate that day
• Catches people who have high sugar only after meals (post-prandial)
• Reflects real-world control, not just clinic-day control

**How often to test:**
• Non-diabetic but at risk: Once a year
• Well-controlled diabetic: Every 6 months
• Poorly controlled: Every 3 months

A simple blood test available at any lab for ₹200-500 across India.
बीमा क्लेम अस्वीकार होने के मुख्य कारण क्या हैं?

बीमा क्लेम अस्वीकार होने के सबसे सामान्य कारण:

**1. Non-disclosure (जानकारी छुपाना)**
• Policy लेते समय पहले से मौजूद बीमारी न बताना
• धूम्रपान या शराब की आदत न बताना

**2. Waiting Period**
• Pre-existing disease का waiting period पूरा न होना
• कुछ बीमारियों का specific waiting period (जैसे मोतियाबिंद — 2 साल)

**3. Exclusions में शामिल**
• Cosmetic surgery, dental, glasses, fertility treatment
• War injuries, self-inflicted injuries
• Experimental treatments

**4. Policy Lapse**
• Premium समय पर न भरने से policy बंद

**5. Documents अधूरे**
• Original bills न देना
• Discharge summary गायब
• डॉक्टर का prescription न होना

**क्लेम अस्वीकार होने पर क्या करें:**
1. Insurance Ombudsman से शिकायत करें (बिल्कुल मुफ्त)
2. IRDAI Bima Bharosa पोर्टल पर complaint — 1800-4254-732
3. Consumer Forum में जाएं
What is the difference between OPD and IPD coverage in health insurance?

**IPD (In-Patient Department)** — Standard Coverage:
• Requires minimum 24 hours hospitalisation
• Covered by all standard health policies
• Includes room, doctor fees, surgery, medicines during stay

**OPD (Out-Patient Department)** — Optional/Add-on:
• Consultations, diagnostics, pharmacy without hospitalisation
• Not covered in basic policies (you pay out of pocket)
• Available as a rider/add-on in many modern policies
• Usually has a sub-limit (e.g., ₹5,000/year OPD cover)

**The gap this creates:**
In India, most healthcare spending is OPD — routine doctor visits, medicines, diagnostics. Basic insurance covers none of this. This is why out-of-pocket spending remains very high even among insured households.

**Policies with OPD cover:**
• Niva Bupa ReAssure 2.0 OPD
• Star Health Comprehensive
• HDFC Ergo Optima Restore (OPD rider)

**Tip**: OPD cover is worth it if you have children (frequent doctor visits) or manage a chronic condition requiring regular consultations.
My creatinine level is 1.8 mg/dL. Is this a concern?

Yes, a creatinine level of 1.8 mg/dL is **above the normal range** and warrants medical attention.

**Normal creatinine ranges:**
• Men: 0.74 – 1.35 mg/dL
• Women: 0.59 – 1.04 mg/dL
• Children: Lower (varies by age)

**1.8 mg/dL suggests**: Mildly to moderately reduced kidney function (early Chronic Kidney Disease stages 2-3)

**Your doctor will likely check:**
• eGFR (estimated Glomerular Filtration Rate) — calculated from creatinine
• Urea / BUN levels
• Urine protein (albumin-to-creatinine ratio)
• Blood pressure (hypertension is both a cause and consequence of CKD)

**Common causes of elevated creatinine:**
• Dehydration (mild, reversible)
• Diabetes-related kidney disease
• Hypertension-related damage
• High protein diet
• Certain medications (NSAIDs, some antibiotics)

**Important**: Please see a nephrologist or physician promptly. At this stage, progression can often be slowed with medication and diet changes.

⚠️ This is general information. A single creatinine value alone does not diagnose kidney disease — trend over time and eGFR calculation are needed.
How does Ayushman Bharat card work at a hospital?

The Ayushman Bharat (PM-JAY) card works as a **cashless, paperless** health card at over 25,000 empanelled hospitals.

**Step-by-step process:**

1. **Verify eligibility**: Check pmjay.gov.in or call 14555 with your Aadhaar
2. **Get your e-card**: Download from pmjay.gov.in or get it printed at a Common Service Centre (CSC)
3. **At the hospital**:
   • Show your Ayushman card + Aadhaar at the Ayushman Bharat help desk
   • They verify your identity biometrically (fingerprint or OTP on Aadhaar-linked mobile)
   • Pre-authorisation is sent to the insurance company
4. **Treatment**: Completely cashless — medicines, tests, surgery, ICU all covered (up to ₹5 lakh)
5. **Discharge**: Sign the discharge form, no payment required for covered treatments

**What is covered:**
• 1,949 procedures including cardiac surgery, cancer treatment, knee replacement
• 3 days pre-hospitalisation, 15 days post-hospitalisation expenses

**What is NOT covered:**
• OPD consultations
• Medicines after discharge
• Cosmetic procedures

**Important**: No premium, no renewal — eligibility is based on SECC 2011 data. Benefits are for the entire family with no family size limit.
What is the generic alternative to Pantoprazole?

Pantoprazole is itself a generic name (the branded version is Pantop, Pan-D, etc.).

**Pantoprazole 40mg** — used for acidity, GERD, stomach ulcers

**Jan Aushadhi price**: ₹3-5 per tablet
**Branded price (Pantop 40)**: ₹8-15 per tablet
**Savings**: 50-70%

**How to get the generic version:**
• Ask your chemist specifically for "Pantoprazole 40mg" (not by brand name)
• Visit the nearest Jan Aushadhi Kendra (Pradhan Mantri Bhartiya Janaushadhi store)
• Available at government hospital pharmacies

**Other options in the same class (PPIs):**
• Omeprazole 20mg (even cheaper, ₹1-2/tablet)
• Rabeprazole 20mg
• Esomeprazole 40mg

All PPIs work similarly for most conditions. Your doctor can advise if switching is appropriate for your situation.
What are the signs of a stroke? What should I do?

🚨 **A stroke is a medical emergency. Call 112 immediately.**

**FAST acronym — Signs of stroke:**
• **F — Face**: One side of the face drooping or numb? Ask them to smile — is it uneven?
• **A — Arms**: Arm weakness? Ask them to raise both arms — does one drift down?
• **S — Speech**: Slurred, strange, or unable to speak? Ask them to repeat a simple sentence
• **T — Time**: Time to call 112. Note the exact time symptoms started.

**Other warning signs:**
• Sudden severe headache with no known cause
• Sudden vision loss in one or both eyes
• Sudden dizziness, loss of balance, difficulty walking
• Sudden numbness in face, arm, or leg (especially on one side)

**What to do:**
1. Call **112** immediately
2. Do NOT give food or water — they may not be able to swallow
3. Do NOT give aspirin unless a doctor specifically tells you to
4. Keep the person calm and lying down (head slightly elevated)
5. Note the time symptoms started — doctors need this for clot-busting treatment (effective only within 4.5 hours)

Every minute counts. Brain cells die at a rate of 1.9 million per minute during a stroke.
What is the difference between term insurance and health insurance?

These are two completely different types of insurance:

**Health Insurance (Mediclaim):**
• **What it covers**: Medical expenses — hospitalisation, surgery, medicines
• **Benefit type**: Pays your hospital bills (reimbursement or cashless)
• **When it pays**: When you are hospitalised or have a covered medical event
• **Premium**: ₹5,000–50,000/year depending on age and sum insured
• **Example**: You are hospitalised for appendix surgery — insurer pays the bill

**Term Life Insurance:**
• **What it covers**: Death — pays a lump sum to your family if you die
• **Benefit type**: Death benefit to nominee
• **When it pays**: Only on the policyholder's death during the policy term
• **Premium**: ₹8,000–20,000/year for ₹1 crore cover (for a 30-year-old non-smoker)
• **Example**: If you die, your family gets ₹1 crore to replace your income

**You need both:**
• Health insurance protects against medical costs during your lifetime
• Term insurance protects your family's financial future if you die

**Priority**: Health insurance first (medical bills can wipe out savings), then term life.
What is Metformin used for? What are the side effects?

**Metformin** is the most commonly prescribed medication for Type 2 Diabetes in India and worldwide.

**Uses:**
• First-line treatment for Type 2 Diabetes
• Also used for PCOS (Polycystic Ovary Syndrome)
• Sometimes used for pre-diabetes to prevent progression

**How it works:**
• Reduces glucose production in the liver
• Improves insulin sensitivity
• Does NOT cause hypoglycaemia (low blood sugar) on its own
• Does NOT cause weight gain (unlike some other diabetes medications)

**Common side effects (especially at start):**
• Nausea, diarrhoea, stomach upset (very common in the first 2-4 weeks)
• Metallic taste in mouth

**Tips to reduce side effects:**
• Always take with food, not on an empty stomach
• Start with a low dose (500mg) and increase gradually
• Extended-release (SR) formulation causes fewer GI side effects

**Rare but serious:**
• Lactic acidosis (very rare — mainly in kidney disease patients)
• Vitamin B12 deficiency with long-term use (get B12 tested annually)

**Generic versions**: Metformin 500mg/850mg/1000mg — ₹5-20/strip at Jan Aushadhi stores

⚠️ Do not stop or change your dose without consulting your doctor.
How to check if a hospital is empanelled under Ayushman Bharat?

There are several ways to find an Ayushman Bharat (PM-JAY) empanelled hospital:

**1. Official website (easiest):**
• Visit: hospitals.pmjay.gov.in
• Search by state, district, hospital name, or speciality
• Filter by public/private, bed strength

**2. Helpline:**
• Call: **14555** (toll-free, available 24/7)
• Tell them your location and medical need — they will guide you

**3. Ayushman app:**
• Download "Ayushman Bharat" app from Google Play / App Store
• Search hospitals by location

**4. At the hospital:**
• Ayushman Bharat empanelled hospitals must display the scheme banner at their entrance
• Look for the yellow and green PM-JAY logo

**5. Common Service Centre (CSC):**
• Your nearest CSC (Jan Seva Kendra) can help you search

**Key tip**: Always confirm with the hospital directly that they are currently active under PM-JAY, as empanelment status can change. Ask specifically about your planned procedure — some hospitals are empanelled for general surgery but not for cardiac or cancer treatments.
My TSH is 6.8 mIU/L. What does this mean?

A TSH of 6.8 mIU/L is **above the normal range** and suggests your thyroid may be underactive (Hypothyroidism).

**Normal TSH range:**
• Standard: 0.4 – 4.0 mIU/L
• Some labs use: 0.5 – 5.0 mIU/L
• Pregnant women: tighter control needed (0.1 – 2.5 mIU/L in first trimester)

**TSH of 6.8 mIU/L typically means:**
• Your pituitary is working harder to stimulate a sluggish thyroid
• This falls in the Subclinical Hypothyroidism range (mildly elevated)

**Symptoms of hypothyroidism to watch for:**
• Fatigue, weight gain
• Feeling cold, dry skin, hair loss
• Constipation, slow heart rate
• Brain fog, depression

**What your doctor will likely check:**
• Free T4 (FT4) — actual thyroid hormone level
• Anti-TPO antibodies — to check for Hashimoto's (autoimmune thyroid disease)
• Repeat TSH in 6-8 weeks to confirm

**Treatment:**
• Some doctors treat above 6, others above 10 (especially without symptoms)
• Levothyroxine (Thyronorm, Eltroxin) is the standard treatment — very affordable

⚠️ Please consult your doctor. A single test result needs clinical context to interpret correctly.
What is the maternity benefit in health insurance?

Maternity coverage in health insurance has specific rules under IRDAI:

**What is typically covered:**
• Normal delivery expenses
• C-section (caesarean) delivery
• Pre and post-natal consultations (in some policies)
• Newborn baby coverage from day 1 (in most modern policies)

**Sub-limits (very important):**
• Normal delivery: ₹15,000 – ₹1,00,000 depending on policy
• C-section: ₹25,000 – ₹2,00,000
• The actual hospital bill often exceeds these limits in private hospitals

**Waiting period:**
• Standard: **9 months to 4 years** waiting period
• This is the most critical issue — buy maternity cover well before planning pregnancy

**Which policies have good maternity cover:**
• Niva Bupa ReAssure 2.0
• Star Women Care Policy
• HDFC Ergo My:Health Women Suraksha

**Employer group insurance:**
• Many corporate group health policies include maternity with no waiting period
• If you have employer cover, check if it includes maternity before buying individual policy

**Tip**: IVF and fertility treatments are generally excluded from standard maternity cover.
What is the Jan Aushadhi scheme? Where can I find a store?

**Pradhan Mantri Bhartiya Janaushadhi Pariyojana (PMBJP)** — commonly called Jan Aushadhi — provides quality generic medicines at 50-90% lower cost than branded medicines.

**How it works:**
• Government procures generic medicines directly from manufacturers
• Sells through dedicated Jan Aushadhi Kendras (stores)
• Same quality standards as branded medicines (manufactured at WHO-GMP certified facilities)
• Currently over **10,500 stores** across India

**What is available:**
• 1,900+ medicines covering all major therapeutic areas
• Surgical items, nutraceuticals
• Sanitary napkins (Suvidha brand) at ₹1/pad

**How to find a store:**
1. Website: janaushadhi.gov.in → Store Locator
2. App: "Jan Aushadhi" on Google Play / App Store
3. Helpline: 1800-180-8080 (toll-free)

**Sample savings:**
• Atorvastatin 10mg: ₹3 vs ₹15 (branded)
• Metformin 500mg: ₹1.50 vs ₹8 (branded)
• Paracetamol 500mg: ₹1 vs ₹3 (branded)

Just ask your chemist for the generic name instead of the brand name — or print your prescription with generic names (doctors are required by MCI to write generic names).
Can you summarise my insurance policy if I upload it?

Yes! Upload your insurance policy PDF using the document upload button in the sidebar, and I will analyse it for you.

I can help you find:
• Sum insured and what it covers
• Waiting periods for specific conditions
• Exclusions and what is NOT covered
• Room rent limits and sub-limits
• Co-payment clauses
• Claim process and network hospitals
• Renewal terms and NCB details

Just upload the PDF and ask your question — for example:
• "What is the waiting period for cardiac surgery?"
• "Are maternity expenses covered?"
• "What is the room rent limit?"
• "Is my policy valid in all states?"
